metadata.title: Cancer Dormancy Recurrence — 15-Year Recurrence and the Cell That Was Waiting

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Merhaba, Liam — in for Bear. Cancer dormancy — 15-year recurrence and the cell that was waiting? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Cancer dormancy — 15-year recurrence and the cell that was waiting?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Cancer dormancy — 15-year recurrence and the cell that was waiting.

A breast cancer patient finishes treatment and is declared disease-free. Fifteen years later she develops bone metastases from the same original tumor. The cells were present in her body the entire time — dormant, invisible to imaging, resistant to therapy. What keeps them dormant? And what wakes them up?

Research cancer dormancy: molecular mechanisms holding disseminated tumor cells dormant, known triggers of reactivation, clinical evidence for dormancy duration in breast, prostate, and melanoma, and therapies that maintain dormancy or prevent reactivation.

props.command: claude "Research cancer dormancy: the molecular mechanisms that hold disseminated tumor cells in a dormant state (TGF-beta2, BMP signaling, urokinase receptor ratio), the known triggers of reactivation (aging bone marrow niche, inflammatory signals, surgery, anesthesia), and the clinical evidence for dormancy duration in breast, prostate, and melanoma. Which therapies have shown ability to maintain dormancy or prevent reactivation?"

beats.narration_text: Python script building three-panel comparison — dormancy mechanism, evidence strength, and clinical strategy.

props.code:
MECHANISMS = [
    {"mechanism": "TGF-b2/p38 MAPK arrest",   "evidence": "STRONG", "druggable": True,
     "strategy": "TGF-b inhibitors (trials: galunisertib)"},
    {"mechanism": "BMP pathway suppression",   "evidence": "MODERATE", "druggable": True,
     "strategy": "BMP7 analogs (preclinical)"},
    {"mechanism": "uPAR:uPA ratio low",        "evidence": "MODERATE", "druggable": False,
     "strategy": "No approved drug"},
    {"mechanism": "Aging bone marrow niche",   "evidence": "STRONG", "druggable": False,
     "strategy": "Exercise + anti-inflammatory (observational)"},
    {"mechanism": "Post-surgical inflammation","evidence": "WEAK", "druggable": True,
     "strategy": "NSAIDs peri-op (pilot trials)"},
]
for m in MECHANISMS:
    drug = "DRUGGABLE" if m["druggable"] else "no drug target"
    print(f"[{m['evidence']:<8}] {m['mechanism']:<30}  {drug}")
    print(f"           Strategy: {m['strategy']}")

beats.narration_text: Three-panel output — dormancy mechanisms with evidence ratings and clinical interventions.
For each reactivation trigger, what is the evidence that it is clinically actionable — is there a trial blocking that trigger in humans, and what happened?

props.command: claude "For each reactivation trigger, what is the evidence that it is clinically actionable — is there a trial blocking that trigger in humans, and what happened?"

beats.narration_text: Revised panel with actionability ratings and trial evidence for each reactivation trigger.

The dormancy state is not passive. The cell is actively suppressing growth signals through TGF-beta and BMP pathways, and those pathways are receptor-mediated and drugable. The problem is that blocking them requires knowing where the dormant cells are — which we cannot do reliably. ctDNA-negative patients may still harbor dormant cells. That gap is the clinical challenge.

Read Sosa et al. 2014 in Nature Reviews Cancer — the most cited review of dormancy mechanisms. Find the table of reactivation triggers with the strongest evidence. Then check whether any of those triggers have a completed randomized trial specifically targeting them for dormancy prevention.
Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
